original_abstract:
While the invasion of Ukraine galvanized Western democracies into a Rare display of unity, the response from the Global South has been characterized by a notable refusal to align with the transatlantic position. Despite widespread condemnation of the conflict in the West, dozens of member states across Africa, Asia, and Latin America have repeatedly abstained from or voted against United Nations resolutions aimed at penalizing Moscow. This paper investigates the motivations behind this apparent defiance, categorizing Global South perspectives into three primary areas: frustration with Western double standards regarding sovereignty, the severe economic fallout caused by the disruption of global development agendas, and the heightened anxiety surrounding nuclear escalation.

Beyond immediate reactions to the war, the analysis explores how the conflict has revitalized a contemporary debate over non-alignment. Moving away from Cold War-era frameworks, many emerging powers are now pursuing "strategic autonomy" or "active non-alignment"—a transactional approach designed to protect national interests amidst intensifying US-China competition. Furthermore, the paper examines how the perceived paralysis of the UN Security Council has provided fresh impetus for Global South-led initiatives to reform multilateral institutions and challenge the hegemony of the US dollar. Ultimately, the study suggests that the war has accelerated a broader shift toward a multipolar order, as developing nations seek to restructure global governance into a more representative and equitable system.
humanized_abstract:
It was in the context of the invasion of Ukraine that the West was able to exhibit a Rare example of unity. The reactions from the Global South were highly differentiated and were characterized by a clear refusal to side with the transatlantic coalition. While in the West the invasion of Ukraine has been almost universally condemned, at the UN in New York dozens of states in Africa, Asia and Latin America have time and again abstained or voted in favour of Russian drafts to prevent the adoption of a resolution aimed at condemning and penalising the violation of Ukrainian sovereignty. Why the Global South has refused to side with the West in this case is complex and multifaceted, but it can be broken down into at least three elements: the perceived western double standard on sovereignty and statehood; the huge damage to the global development agenda and the huge potential harm to the poorest peoples due to the current sanctions regime; and the existential threat posed by nuclear war and the fear that a direct military confrontation between the US and Russia may have already begun.

The war in Ukraine will dominate the headlines in the coming weeks. This briefing is more concerned with the longer-term implications for the Global South’s position in international affairs and the global order. The Ukraine war is reviving debates on non-alignment. Most Cold War vocabulary has long lost its meaning. Many countries in the Global South are looking to achieve “strategic autonomy” or are advocating for an approach they call “active non-alignment”, trying to maximise their interests in the current era of increasing US-China rivalry. And while the inefficiencies and stalemates of the UN Security Council will probably continue, the war has revived momentum in the Global South for reforms to the multilateral system and to reduce the role of the US dollar as the currency of choice for trade and investment. Overall, the war is accelerating the multipolarisation of the world and the Global South’s efforts to rebalance the global order in favour of an arrangement that is more distributed, more equal and more democratic.
